title: What is the difference between HashMap and Hashtable?

answers.junior: HashMap and Hashtable are both Map implementations that store key-value pairs using hash-based indexing, but they differ in several critical ways. The most important difference is thread safety: Hashtable is synchronized on every method, making it thread-safe but significantly slower in single-threaded contexts; HashMap is unsynchronized and performs better but requires external synchronization if used concurrently.
answers.middle: HashMap and Hashtable are both Map implementations that store key-value pairs using hash-based indexing, but they differ in several critical ways. The most important difference is thread safety: Hashtable is synchronized on every method, making it thread-safe but significantly slower in single-threaded contexts; HashMap is unsynchronized and performs better but requires external synchronization if used concurrently. HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values, whereas Hashtable throws NullPointerException if either key or value is null. HashMap is part of the Java Collections Framework (introduced in Java 2) and extends AbstractMap, while Hashtable is a legacy class from Java 1.0 that extends Dictionary and was retrofitted to implement Map. In terms of iteration, HashMap uses an Iterator (fail-fast), while Hashtable uses Enumeration (not fail-fast) as its legacy mechanism.
answers.senior: HashMap and Hashtable are both Map implementations that store key-value pairs using hash-based indexing, but they differ in several critical ways. The most important difference is thread safety: Hashtable is synchronized on every method, making it thread-safe but significantly slower in single-threaded contexts; HashMap is unsynchronized and performs better but requires external synchronization if used concurrently. HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values, whereas Hashtable throws NullPointerException if either key or value is null. HashMap is part of the Java Collections Framework (introduced in Java 2) and extends AbstractMap, while Hashtable is a legacy class from Java 1.0 that extends Dictionary and was retrofitted to implement Map. In terms of iteration, HashMap uses an Iterator (fail-fast), while Hashtable uses Enumeration (not fail-fast) as its legacy mechanism. Performance-wise, HashMap is generally faster due to lack of synchronization overhead, and its initial capacity and load factor (default 0.75) can be tuned for performance. For concurrent use cases, the recommended modern alternatives are ConcurrentHashMap (high-performance, segment-level locking in Java 7, CAS-based in Java 8+) or Collections.synchronizedMap(new HashMap<>()) for simple wrapping. In practice, Hashtable should be considered deprecated for new code; HashMap or ConcurrentHashMap should always be preferred. One subtle difference is that HashMap's iteration order is undefined and may change on resize, while both share the same O(1) average-case complexity for get/put operations assuming a good hash function and low collision rate.
